text.title: Active Listening Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules grounding the RASA practice. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Every substantive conversation MUST cover all four RASA steps in order: Receive (full attention, no phone), Appreciate (verbal acknowledgment), Summarize (paraphrase back), Ask (one open follow-up).

rationale.source: Center for Creative Leadership — RASA Framework

rule.rationale: Skipping Summarize is the most common failure; without paraphrasing, the speaker cannot confirm understanding and will either repeat or withhold detail.

rule.statement: Questions in the generated script MUST open with What, How, or Tell-me-about. Openers Is, Do, Did, Are, Were, Will, Would are forbidden because they produce yes/no answers.
rule.rationale: Closed questions cap exploration; open questions give the speaker control of scope and surface information the interviewer did not know to ask about.

rule.statement: For high-stakes conversations the script MUST include at least one explicit reflective-listening prompt of the form "It sounds like you're [FEELING] because [CONTENT]." targeting Level 5 empathic listening.

rationale.source: Stephen R. Covey — 7 Habits of Highly Effective People

rule.rationale: Levels 1-4 process words; Level 5 processes meaning and feeling. Decision-makers under stress communicate 40-60% of their actual concern through emotional register, not explicit words.

rule.statement: Reflective statements MUST use the formula "It sounds like you're [FEELING] because [CONTENT]." Pre-generated reflections without observed signal context are forbidden — the script must mark them as response-only, not opener.
rule.rationale: Naming the feeling signals Level 5 attention; the speaker either confirms (and feels understood) or corrects (providing higher-quality signal). Out-of-context reflection lands as condescending.

rule.statement: Mirroring prompts MUST repeat the last 1-3 words of the speaker as a question and MUST NOT add interpretation. Generated examples must not embed assumptions about why the speaker said it.

rationale.source: Chris Voss — Never Split the Difference

rule.rationale: Mirroring is the lowest-friction technique with the highest elaboration rate. Adding interpretation converts it into a leading question and kills the elaboration effect.

rule.statement: The script MUST include an explicit "pause 3-5 seconds after speaker finishes" cue before any follow-up. For async/text contexts the substitute MUST be a "Is there anything you'd like to add?" prompt because text cannot encode silence.
rule.rationale: People add the most important information after the first pause. Immediate response signals waiting-to-speak rather than listening. In text the explicit second-pass prompt closes the same gap.
